text: Hello, this is a case of laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to separate the omentum, which is visibly adhered to the anterior abdominal wall, using laparoscopic instruments.
This patient has a two centimeter stone in the gallbladder. And she was symptomatic with few episodes of the pain in the past.
She has been the IVF performed and after that she has the acute cholecystitis.
The surgeon opens the posterior peritoneum, revealing the roviate sulcus below, which is identified as a safe area for dissection.

The surgeon positions the silicon jaw of the harmonic scalpel towards the cystic duct to prevent heat transfer, then uses the tip of the harmonic to gently open the anterior peritoneum at the same level.

The surgeon performs skeletonization to identify the Calot's triangle, cystic duct, and cystic artery. The inferior surface of the liver forms one side of the triangle. The tenting of the cystic duct and artery is visible as the peritoneum over these structures is carefully skeletonized.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to create a posterior window between the cystic duct and cystic artery after stripping the peritoneum and clearing adventitious tissue.

The surgeon uses the Maryland dissector to create an anterior window between the cystic duct and cystic artery. The indentation by the tip and the opening of the full jaw of the Maryland instrument form a window approximately 2.2 centimeters wide, allowing for the placement of a clip and further dissection of the cystic duct and artery. The windows between these structures are clearly visible.
The surgeon hooks the cystic artery from below to create a proper posterior window, further defining Calot's triangle. The cystic duct is then clipped using surgical clips.
The surgeon applies titanium clips over the cystic duct and cystic artery using a clip applicator, with both tips of the applicator clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon applies a single titanium clip to the cystic duct and artery, ensuring no leakage occurs. Using harmonic energy, the cystic duct and artery are dissected. The gallbladder is then separated from the liver with anterolateral and anteromedial traction.

The surgeon maintains proper traction on the Hartmann's pouch, positioning the neck of the gallbladder towards the falciform ligament. Anterolateral traction is applied to facilitate peritoneal dissection, and the body of the gallbladder is pulled anteriorly, gradually separating it from the liver.

This is just a simple case of laparoscopic cholecystectomy, which will not take more than ten minutes time to perform with the minimum bleeding. Patient has advantage that can be discharged in the day care the same day.
The surgeon applies anteromedial and anterolateral traction to open the peritoneum, revealing a clear areolar plane. The body of the gallbladder is progressively separated from the liver.

The surgeon examines the titanium clips applied to the cystic duct and cystic artery before proceeding to separate the remaining part of the gallbladder from the liver using gentle traction while lifting the liver.
The surgeon addresses the final attachment of the gallbladder to the liver, revealing a gallbladder containing a stone that has been completely removed.

The surgeon uses a claw forceps inserted through the epigastric port to grasp the gallbladder. An ovum forceps is then inserted into the gallbladder to crush the stone inside, facilitating its removal.
Thank you very much for watching this video.
